module: Module 5 - Blood Banking and Transfusion Medicine

questions.question: An individual with blood type O has which antigens on their red blood cells?
choices: Neither A nor B antigens (H antigen only)
questions.answer: Neither A nor B antigens (H antigen only)
questions.explanation: Blood type O individuals lack A and B antigens on their RBCs (only H antigen is present). They have both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their serum. Type O is the universal RBC donor because of the absence of A and B antigens.

questions.question: The universal plasma donor blood type is:
questions.answer: AB positive
questions.explanation: AB individuals have no ABO antibodies in their plasma (neither anti-A nor anti-B), making AB plasma the universal donor plasma. O negative is the universal RBC donor.

questions.question: The Rh system's most clinically significant antigen (D antigen) is important because Rh-negative individuals who receive Rh-positive blood:
choices: Will immediately hemolyze the Rh-positive blood via naturally occurring antibodies
Can develop anti-D (immune) antibodies with subsequent exposure causing hemolytic reaction
questions.answer: Can develop anti-D (immune) antibodies with subsequent exposure causing hemolytic reaction
questions.explanation: Anti-D is an immune antibody (IgG) — formed only after exposure to D antigen via transfusion or pregnancy. First exposure causes sensitization; subsequent exposure with Rh+ blood causes hemolytic transfusion reaction. First exposure itself is usually uneventful.

questions.question: Hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN) due to Rh incompatibility is prevented by administering:
choices: Rh immune globulin (RhIG/RhoGAM) to Rh-negative mothers
questions.answer: Rh immune globulin (RhIG/RhoGAM) to Rh-negative mothers
questions.explanation: RhIG (anti-D) is given to Rh-negative women at 28 weeks gestation and within 72 hours postpartum if the baby is Rh-positive. It prevents sensitization by destroying fetal D-positive cells before the mother's immune system can form anti-D antibodies.

questions.question: The ABO antibodies (anti-A and anti-B) are primarily of which immunoglobulin class and how are they acquired?
choices: IgM; naturally occurring, produced without prior RBC exposure (from environmental antigens)
questions.answer: IgM; naturally occurring, produced without prior RBC exposure (from environmental antigens)
questions.explanation: ABO antibodies are 'naturally occurring' IgM antibodies formed by 3–6 months of age, stimulated by exposure to A and B-like antigens on environmental bacteria/food. They are cold-reactive and potent complement activators causing acute hemolysis.

questions.question: In forward (cell) grouping, patient RBCs are tested against commercial anti-A and anti-B antisera. In reverse (serum) grouping, patient serum is tested against known A1 and B cells. Discrepancy occurs when:
choices: The forward and reverse typing results are inconsistent with each other
Only the forward typing is performed
The patient is blood type AB
questions.answer: The forward and reverse typing results are inconsistent with each other
questions.explanation: An ABO discrepancy means forward and reverse typing disagree. Causes include: patient age (<4 months or elderly), subgroups of A or B, disease (leukemia, hypogammaglobulinemia), polyagglutination, or technical errors. Must be resolved before transfusion.

questions.question: Which blood component is stored at 1–6°C for up to 35–42 days?
questions.answer: Packed red blood cells (pRBC)
questions.explanation: pRBCs are stored at 1–6°C in AS-1 or CPDA-1 additive solution for 35–42 days. FFP is stored at ≤-18°C for 1 year. Platelets are stored at 20–24°C with continuous agitation for 5 days. Cryoprecipitate is stored at ≤-18°C for 1 year.

questions.question: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) is indicated for:
choices: Replacement of multiple coagulation factor deficiencies or reversal of warfarin
questions.answer: Replacement of multiple coagulation factor deficiencies or reversal of warfarin
questions.explanation: FFP contains all coagulation factors, fibrinogen, and plasma proteins. Indicated for: coagulopathy with active bleeding (liver disease, DIC), warfarin reversal when Vitamin K is too slow, TTP (as replacement in plasmapheresis).

questions.question: Cryoprecipitate is the cold-precipitable fraction of FFP. It is rich in which coagulation factors?
choices: Factors II, VII, IX, X (vitamin K-dependent factors)
Fibrinogen, Factor VIII, von Willebrand factor (vWF), Factor XIII, and fibronectin
All plasma proteins in concentrated form
questions.answer: Fibrinogen, Factor VIII, von Willebrand factor (vWF), Factor XIII, and fibronectin
questions.explanation: Cryoprecipitate is prepared by thawing FFP at 1–6°C and collecting the precipitate. It contains: Fibrinogen (~250 mg), Factor VIII (~80 IU), vWF, Factor XIII, fibronectin. Used for hypofibrinogenemia, DIC, hemophilia A (when concentrates unavailable).

questions.question: The major crossmatch tests patient's SERUM against donor's CELLS. The minor crossmatch tests:
choices: Donor's cells against a third-party serum
Patient's serum against patient's own cells (autologous control)
questions.answer: Patient's cells against donor's serum
questions.explanation: Major crossmatch (most important): patient serum × donor RBCs — detects patient antibodies against donor antigens. Minor crossmatch: donor serum × patient RBCs — detects donor antibodies against patient antigens (less critical since donor plasma is diluted).

questions.question: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR) is most commonly caused by:
choices: ABO incompatibility due to clerical/identification errors
Bacterial contamination of the blood unit
questions.answer: ABO incompatibility due to clerical/identification errors
questions.explanation: AHTR is most commonly caused by ABO incompatibility from clerical errors (wrong patient labeled, wrong unit given). Anti-A or anti-B (IgM) activates complement → intravascular hemolysis → fever, chills, back/flank pain, hemoglobinuria, DIC. Fatal if uncorrected.

questions.question: The type and screen procedure:
choices: Performs a complete crossmatch for every unit of blood
Determines ABO/Rh type and screens for unexpected antibodies; crossmatch is done only if transfusion is needed
Tests for all possible alloantibodies by extended antigen typing
Is equivalent to a type and crossmatch
questions.answer: Determines ABO/Rh type and screens for unexpected antibodies; crossmatch is done only if transfusion is needed
questions.explanation: Type and screen: ABO/Rh typing + antibody screen using panel cells. If screen is negative, an electronic (computer) crossmatch or immediate spin crossmatch suffices when transfusion is ordered. Reduces time and cost compared to full crossmatch for every patient.

questions.question: The Bombay phenotype (Oh) is characterized by:
choices: Absence of H antigen and presence of anti-H, anti-A, anti-B in serum
questions.answer: Absence of H antigen and presence of anti-H, anti-A, anti-B in serum
questions.explanation: Bombay phenotype (hh): mutation in FUT1 gene → no H antigen synthesized → no A or B antigens (H is the precursor). Serum contains potent anti-H, anti-A, anti-B. Can only receive blood from other Bombay donors. Occurs in ~1:10,000 in India.

questions.question: Platelet concentrates are stored at 20–24°C with continuous gentle agitation. The risk of this storage condition compared to refrigeration is:
choices: Higher risk of bacterial contamination and growth (especially gram-positive organisms)
questions.answer: Higher risk of bacterial contamination and growth (especially gram-positive organisms)
questions.explanation: Room temperature storage optimizes platelet viability but allows bacterial proliferation. Staphylococcus epidermidis and other gram-positive organisms are most common. Bacterial sepsis is the most common transfusion-related infectious fatality. Bacterial testing by culture is mandatory for platelets.

questions.question: Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction (FNHTR) is caused by:
choices: Cytokines accumulated during storage and recipient antibodies against donor leukocytes
questions.answer: Cytokines accumulated during storage and recipient antibodies against donor leukocytes
questions.explanation: FNHTR: temperature rise ≥1°C during transfusion. Caused by recipient HLA or granulocyte-specific antibodies reacting with donor WBCs, or cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF) released from WBCs during storage. Prevented by leukoreduction.

questions.question: Which test detects weak D (Du) antigen in individuals who test negative by routine D typing?
choices: Indirect antiglobulin test with incubation at 37°C
Saline tube test at room temperature
questions.answer: Indirect antiglobulin test with incubation at 37°C
questions.explanation: Weak D (formerly Du) antigen has fewer D antigen sites. Routine serological D typing may be negative. Weak D is detected by IAT (incubation with anti-D at 37°C + AHG). Weak D individuals are usually treated as Rh-positive donors and Rh-negative recipients.

questions.question: The Kidd blood group system is clinically important because Kidd antibodies (anti-Jka, anti-Jkb) are notorious for:
choices: Being evanescent (dropping below detectable levels) and causing delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions
Crossing the placenta causing HDN only
questions.answer: Being evanescent (dropping below detectable levels) and causing delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions
questions.explanation: Kidd antibodies are notorious for being evanescent — they can drop below detectable levels between transfusions, causing a false-negative antibody screen. Upon re-exposure (anamnestic response), they rapidly rise and cause delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions (DHTR) 3–14 days post-transfusion.

questions.question: Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) is characterized by:
choices: Fever, hypotension, and non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hours of transfusion
questions.answer: Fever, hypotension, and non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hours of transfusion
questions.explanation: TRALI is a life-threatening complication presenting with acute respiratory distress, hypoxia (PaO2/FiO2 <300), and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates within 6 hours of transfusion. It is the leading cause of transfusion-related mortality. Caused by donor anti-HLA or anti-neutrophil antibodies.

questions.question: Which ABO blood type is the universal recipient for red blood cells?
questions.answer: AB positive
questions.explanation: AB positive individuals have both A and B antigens on their RBCs and are Rh-positive. They have no ABO antibodies (cannot reject A, B, or O blood) and have no anti-D (accept Rh+ blood). AB+ is the universal RBC recipient.

questions.question: Leukoreduction (leukocyte depletion) of blood components is performed to prevent all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.answer: Bacterial sepsis from platelet transfusion
questions.explanation: Leukoreduction reduces FNHTR, CMV transmission (virus hides in WBCs), and HLA alloimmunization. It does NOT prevent bacterial sepsis from platelets (bacteria are not in WBCs). Bacterial sepsis prevention requires bacterial testing of platelets.

questions.question: The antibody screen (indirect antiglobulin test) uses 2–3 reagent red cells selected to express which antigens?
choices: All common clinically significant antigens (D, C, c, E, e, K, Jka, Jkb, Fya, Fyb, S, s)
questions.answer: All common clinically significant antigens (D, C, c, E, e, K, Jka, Jkb, Fya, Fyb, S, s)
questions.explanation: Screening cells express clinically significant antigens (Rh, Kell, Duffy, Kidd, MNS, Lewis, P systems) in a homozygous or heterozygous pattern. A positive screen indicates an unexpected alloantibody that must be identified before transfusion.

questions.question: Autologous blood donation is the safest transfusion option because:
choices: It eliminates the risk of alloimmunization, infection transmission, and incompatibility reactions
It is cheaper than allogenic blood
questions.answer: It eliminates the risk of alloimmunization, infection transmission, and incompatibility reactions
questions.explanation: Autologous (self-donated) blood eliminates risks of alloantibody formation, viral transmission (HIV, HBV, HCV), and transfusion reactions. However, it still carries risks of bacterial contamination and clerical errors. Volume may be insufficient for major surgery.

questions.question: The direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive in all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: Pre-transfusion testing of normal healthy donors
questions.answer: Pre-transfusion testing of normal healthy donors
questions.explanation: A positive DAT indicates antibody or complement is coating the patient's RBCs in vivo (AIHA, HDN, drug-induced, delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction). Healthy blood donors have no in vivo RBC sensitization — their DAT is negative.

questions.question: The elution technique (acid elution, heat elution) in immunohematology is used to:
choices: Wash RBCs free of unbound proteins
Remove and recover antibody from sensitized RBCs for further testing
Store blood components at cold temperatures
questions.answer: Remove and recover antibody from sensitized RBCs for further testing
questions.explanation: Elution dissociates antibodies from sensitized RBCs (DAT-positive cells). The eluate is then tested against panel cells to identify the antibody specificity. Used in AIHA workup, HDN investigation, and transfusion reaction workup.

questions.question: The Kleihauer-Betke test is performed to determine the dose of RhIG after delivery. The test quantifies the volume of feto-maternal hemorrhage (FMH). The standard postpartum dose of RhIG protects against FMH of how much fetal blood?
choices: 30 mL of fetal whole blood (or 15 mL fetal RBCs)
questions.answer: 30 mL of fetal whole blood (or 15 mL fetal RBCs)
questions.explanation: One vial of RhIG (300 μg) protects against 30 mL of fetal whole blood (or 15 mL of fetal RBCs). If KB test indicates >30 mL FMH, additional RhIG vials are needed. Calculation: volume of FMH (mL) ÷ 30, rounded up to nearest whole number = number of vials.

questions.question: Cold agglutinin disease is caused by IgM antibodies that react best at 4°C. The most common specificity of the cold agglutinin in Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection is:
questions.answer: Anti-I
questions.explanation: Cold agglutinins in Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection are anti-I IgM antibodies. Anti-i is associated with infectious mononucleosis (EBV). Anti-I causes in vitro agglutination at cold temperatures and can cause hemolysis in cold extremities.

questions.question: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR) typically occurs:
choices: During or immediately after transfusion (<1 hour)
3–14 days after transfusion due to anamnestic antibody response
questions.answer: 3–14 days after transfusion due to anamnestic antibody response
questions.explanation: DHTR occurs when an antibody level is too low to detect pre-transfusion but rises after re-exposure (anamnestic response). Transfused RBCs are then slowly destroyed (extravascular hemolysis). Patients present with unexplained drop in Hb, jaundice, and positive DAT days after transfusion.

questions.question: The Kell blood group system contains the K (KEL1) antigen. Anti-K is clinically significant because it:
choices: Causes hemolytic transfusion reactions and HDN and suppresses erythropoiesis
Is naturally occurring without prior exposure
questions.answer: Causes hemolytic transfusion reactions and HDN and suppresses erythropoiesis
questions.explanation: Anti-K is an immune IgG alloantibody formed after transfusion or pregnancy. It causes hemolytic transfusion reactions and severe HDN (suppresses fetal erythropoiesis in addition to hemolysis). K antigen is present in only ~9% of the population.

questions.question: In neonatal exchange transfusion for HDN due to anti-D, the blood used should be:
choices: O positive compatible with mother's ABO type
O Rh-negative, crossmatched against the mother's serum
questions.answer: O Rh-negative, crossmatched against the mother's serum
questions.explanation: For HDN exchange transfusion: use O Rh-negative pRBCs (lacks the offending D antigen and ABO antigens) crossmatched against the mother's serum (which contains the causative anti-D). Fresh blood (<7 days) in irradiated, CMV-negative units is preferred.

questions.question: The blood component used to treat TTP (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura) via therapeutic plasma exchange is:
choices: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) as replacement fluid
questions.answer: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) as replacement fluid
questions.explanation: TTP is treated by therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) using FFP as the replacement fluid. TPE removes the inhibitory antibody against ADAMTS13 and replaces it with functional ADAMTS13 from donor FFP. Platelet transfusion is CONTRAINDICATED in TTP (worsens clotting).

questions.question: An irradiated blood component is required for which patient population to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GvHD)?
choices: Immunocompromised patients (bone marrow transplant, severe combined immunodeficiency, congenital T cell defects)
questions.answer: Immunocompromised patients (bone marrow transplant, severe combined immunodeficiency, congenital T cell defects)
questions.explanation: TA-GvHD occurs when immunocompetent donor T lymphocytes engraft in an immunocompromised host and attack host tissues. Gamma irradiation (25 Gy) inactivates T lymphocytes. At-risk: BMT patients, SCID, intrauterine transfusions, patients receiving HLA-matched blood.

questions.question: Washed red blood cells are prepared by removing plasma proteins. They are indicated for:
choices: Patients with severe IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibodies (anaphylaxis risk)
Patients with cold agglutinin disease only
questions.answer: Patients with severe IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibodies (anaphylaxis risk)
questions.explanation: IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA antibodies can develop severe anaphylactic reactions to plasma proteins (IgA in donor blood). Washed RBCs or IgA-deficient donors are used. Washed cells also remove allergenic proteins, antibodies, potassium, and microaggregates.

questions.question: The Duffy blood group antigens (Fya, Fyb) are important because Duffy-null individuals (Fy(a-b-)) are resistant to infection by:
questions.answer: Plasmodium vivax
questions.explanation: Plasmodium vivax uses the Duffy antigen (DARC — Duffy Antigen Receptor for Chemokines) as its RBC receptor. Individuals with the Duffy-null phenotype Fy(a-b-) (common in West Africans) are resistant to P. vivax malaria, explaining its rarity in West Africa.

questions.question: Hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN) due to ABO incompatibility is usually LESS severe than Rh HDN because:
choices: Anti-A and anti-B do not cross the placenta
A and B antigens are found on many tissues (not just RBCs), diluting antibody effect, and fetal RBCs have fewer A/B sites
ABO HDN only occurs with blood type A mothers
Rh antibodies are IgM and cannot cause hemolysis
questions.answer: A and B antigens are found on many tissues (not just RBCs), diluting antibody effect, and fetal RBCs have fewer A/B sites
questions.explanation: ABO HDN: anti-A/anti-B IgG crosses the placenta, but fetal RBCs express fewer A/B antigens than adult cells, and A/B antigens on other fetal tissues absorb antibody, reducing hemolysis. ABO HDN rarely requires exchange transfusion, unlike severe Rh HDN.

questions.question: Compatibility testing sequence from least to most stringent is:
choices: Electronic crossmatch → immediate spin → Coombs/IAT crossmatch
Immediate spin → Coombs crossmatch → electronic crossmatch
Coombs crossmatch → immediate spin → electronic crossmatch
questions.answer: Electronic crossmatch → immediate spin → Coombs/IAT crossmatch
questions.explanation: Electronic (computer) crossmatch: most efficient for patients with no alloantibodies, using electronic verification of ABO compatibility. Immediate spin crossmatch: detects ABO incompatibility at RT. Coombs (IAT) crossmatch: detects unexpected alloantibodies; most thorough.

questions.question: The indirect antiglobulin phase (Coombs phase) of the crossmatch is performed at which temperature?
questions.explanation: The IAT (Coombs) crossmatch incubates patient serum + donor RBCs at 37°C (physiological temperature) to detect IgG alloantibodies that react at body temperature. After incubation, cells are washed and AHG (Coombs reagent) is added.

questions.question: Group O blood is the preferred blood for emergency transfusion when the patient's blood type is unknown. If a female of childbearing age, which type must be given?
choices: O positive is acceptable for all ages
questions.answer: O negative
questions.explanation: O negative blood must be given to females of childbearing potential (regardless of age) to prevent Rh sensitization. If a woman receives Rh-positive blood and develops anti-D, future Rh-positive pregnancies could result in severe HDN.

questions.question: The ABO subgroup A1 has more A antigen sites per cell than A2. Clinically, the distinction is important because some A2 individuals:
choices: Cannot receive any type of blood
Develop anti-A1 (a clinically significant antibody that can cause transfusion reactions)
questions.answer: Develop anti-A1 (a clinically significant antibody that can cause transfusion reactions)
questions.explanation: ~1–8% of A2 individuals and ~22–26% of A2B individuals produce anti-A1, which can react at 37°C and cause hemolytic transfusion reactions if A1 blood is given. A2 patients with clinically significant anti-A1 should receive A2 or O blood.

questions.question: Pathogen reduction technology (PRT/PRT) for blood products uses:
choices: Psoralen + UVA light (amotosalen) or riboflavin + UV light to inactivate nucleic acids of pathogens
questions.answer: Psoralen + UVA light (amotosalen) or riboflavin + UV light to inactivate nucleic acids of pathogens
questions.explanation: Pathogen reduction technologies (e.g., INTERCEPT using amotosalen/UVA, Mirasol using riboflavin/UV) inactivate viruses, bacteria, and parasites in platelets and plasma by cross-linking nucleic acids. These reduce transfusion-transmitted infections and can replace bacterial testing of platelets.

questions.question: Massive transfusion is defined as transfusion of ≥10 units of pRBCs in 24 hours. The dilutional coagulopathy in massive transfusion is treated with:
choices: Balanced resuscitation with 1:1:1 ratio of pRBCs:FFP:platelets, plus cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen
questions.answer: Balanced resuscitation with 1:1:1 ratio of pRBCs:FFP:platelets, plus cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen
questions.explanation: In massive transfusion/hemorrhage (trauma, PPH), dilutional coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, and hypofibrinogenemia develop. Balanced resuscitation (1:1:1 ratio) mimics whole blood. Cryoprecipitate provides fibrinogen when levels <100 mg/dL.

questions.question: The Lewis blood group antigens (Lea, Leb) are unique because they are:
choices: Soluble plasma antigens adsorbed onto RBC surfaces
questions.answer: Soluble plasma antigens adsorbed onto RBC surfaces
questions.explanation: Lewis antigens are synthesized in epithelial cells and secreted as soluble glycolipids in body fluids and plasma. They are passively adsorbed onto RBC surfaces, not synthesized directly on RBCs. This explains why Lewis antigens can change with blood transfusion or pregnancy.

questions.question: The most common cause of transfusion-related death in the Philippines and globally is:
questions.answer: TACO (transfusion-associated circulatory overload)
questions.explanation: TACO has become the leading cause of transfusion-related fatality in many countries. Risk factors: elderly, cardiac disease, renal failure, excessive transfusion rate. Presents with acute pulmonary edema, hypertension, and positive fluid balance. Prevention: slow transfusion rate, diuretics.

questions.question: Granulocyte transfusions are indicated for:
choices: Severe neutropenia (<0.5 × 10^9/L) with bacterial or fungal infection not responding to antimicrobials
questions.answer: Severe neutropenia (<0.5 × 10^9/L) with bacterial or fungal infection not responding to antimicrobials
questions.explanation: Granulocyte transfusions are reserved for patients with profound neutropenia with documented infection unresponsive to standard antimicrobials. Due to short shelf life (<24 hours at 20–24°C), concurrent GCSF stimulation of donor improves yield.

questions.question: The standard hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN) workup on a cord blood sample includes:
choices: ABO/Rh typing and direct antiglobulin test (DAT)
Complete CBC and full antibody panel only
Crossmatch with maternal serum and Western blot
questions.answer: ABO/Rh typing and direct antiglobulin test (DAT)
questions.explanation: Cord blood HDN workup: ABO and Rh typing + DAT. A positive DAT indicates maternal antibody coating the neonatal RBCs. The elution identifies the antibody specificity. CBC, bilirubin, reticulocyte count help assess severity.

questions.question: Which law governs blood banking practice and the regulation of blood service facilities in the Philippines?
choices: Republic Act 7719 (National Blood Services Act of 1994)
questions.answer: Republic Act 7719 (National Blood Services Act of 1994)
questions.explanation: RA 7719 (National Blood Services Act of 1994) governs blood banking in the Philippines. It established the National Voluntary Blood Services Program, mandates voluntary non-remunerated blood donation, and regulates blood service facilities including blood banks.

questions.question: The Rh system has 5 major antigens: D, C, c, E, and e. An individual typed as DCcEe is:
choices: Heterozygous for both C/c and E/e, and D-positive
D negative with C and E antigens
questions.answer: Heterozygous for both C/c and E/e, and D-positive
questions.explanation: An individual typed as DCcEe has: D antigen (Rh-positive), both C and c antigens (heterozygous C/c), and both E and e antigens (heterozygous E/e). This is the most common Rh phenotype in most populations.

questions.question: Hyperhemolysis syndrome is a severe delayed hemolytic reaction where patient's own RBCs are also destroyed. It is most common in:
choices: Sickle cell disease patients receiving chronic transfusions
questions.answer: Sickle cell disease patients receiving chronic transfusions
questions.explanation: Hyperhemolysis syndrome occurs primarily in sickle cell disease patients. The Hgb drops below pre-transfusion levels due to destruction of both donor and patient RBCs. Mechanism involves bystander hemolysis. Avoid further transfusion if possible; IVIG and steroids may help.

questions.question: In blood bank serology, a 'panreactive' antibody screen — where all panel cells react — most commonly indicates:
choices: High-frequency alloantibody, warm autoantibody (AIHA), or multiple alloantibodies
questions.answer: High-frequency alloantibody, warm autoantibody (AIHA), or multiple alloantibodies
questions.explanation: A panreactive antibody screen (all cells react) suggests: (1) antibody against a high-frequency antigen (present on virtually all donor cells), (2) warm autoantibody in AIHA, or (3) multiple alloantibodies. Adsorption techniques and antigen-negative rare donors may be required.

questions.question: The 2-mercaptoethanol (2-ME) or dithiothreitol (DTT) treatment disrupts IgM pentamers by cleaving disulfide bonds. This technique is used to:
choices: Distinguish IgM from IgG antibodies; IgM reactivity is abolished by 2-ME/DTT treatment
questions.answer: Distinguish IgM from IgG antibodies; IgM reactivity is abolished by 2-ME/DTT treatment
questions.explanation: 2-ME and DTT cleave the disulfide bonds holding IgM subunits together, inactivating IgM antibodies. If reactivity disappears after treatment, the antibody is IgM. If reactivity persists, it is IgG (no disulfide bonds between subunits). Used in syphilis serology and Rh antibody work.

questions.question: Philippine Blood Coordinating Council (PBCC) standards require that all blood collected from donors be tested for which infectious disease markers?
choices: HIV, HBsAg, anti-HCV, malaria, and syphilis
HIV, HBsAg, anti-HCV, syphilis, and Chagas disease
questions.answer: HIV, HBsAg, anti-HCV, malaria, and syphilis
questions.explanation: Philippine blood banking standards (DOH/PBCC) require testing all donated blood for: HBsAg (Hepatitis B), anti-HCV (Hepatitis C), HIV (anti-HIV 1/2), malaria (thick blood smear or RDT), and syphilis (RPR/VDRL). This reflects the endemic infectious diseases in the Philippines.
